notes: Blueprints are contender/finalist profiles, not exclusively championship winners. Add true title teams (e.g. 2022 Warriors, 2023 Nuggets, 2024 Celtics) to enrich comparison power. Stats sourced from ESPN Hollinger unless noted.

blueprints.source_file: 2023–24 Dallas Mavericks (Post-Trade Deadline Version) – Team-Building Blueprint.pdf
blueprints.identity_sentence: Superstar-centric 5-out motion offense built around Dončić/Irving, paired with length-and-switch defense anchored by dual rim protectors.
blueprints.offensive_system: 5-out motion (Delay-Chicago actions, constant pin-downs and DHOs, star-led improvisation)
blueprints.defensive_system: Switch/drop hybrid with dual rim protectors; versatile wings switch above break, bigs drop and protect paint

stats.notes: Team-level box stats not fully extracted from this blueprint doc format. Playoff rim defense stat is explicitly sourced.

construction_principles: Build spacing around a generational playmaker — 4 perimeter players + 1 screener/roller at all times
Require a true secondary creator (not just a shooter) to punish help defense
Pair switchable wings (6'6"–6'8") with mobile rim-protecting bigs — the combination, not either alone, creates the defensive identity
Trade deadline flexibility: willing to sacrifice perimeter shooting to add defensive infrastructure mid-season
Role players have one job: shoot, cut, or screen — no secondary creation expected from role depth

archetypes.label: Generational playmaker / focal point
archetypes.draft_note: System is entirely built around this archetype — cannot be emulated without a Luka-caliber player

archetypes.label: Off-ball scorer / secondary playmaker
fit_indicators: capable of PnR when starter rests
archetypes.draft_note: Critical gap filler — offense collapses to one-dimensional without this archetype

archetypes.label: Switchable perimeter defender + spot-up shooter
archetypes.draft_note: Need 2+ of these; system breaks without floor spacing around the stars

archetypes.label: Corner/wing shooter, non-creator
archetypes.draft_note: Acquired at trade deadline specifically to maintain spacing while adding length

archetypes.label: Mobile big, lob finisher, paint anchor
archetypes.draft_note: Two rim protectors is non-negotiable for this system — one starts, one provides backup interior defense

structural_weaknesses: High shooting variance — cold shooting nights expose lack of secondary actions
Superstar-dependent: if star creator is blitzed or injured, offense has no fallback generator
Limited decision-makers beyond top-2: teams that physically match up and blitz Dončić can stifle the offense

blueprints.source_file: 2024–25 Indiana Pacers Team Identity and Playstyle Profile for Draft Modeling.pdf
blueprints.identity_sentence: High-efficiency ball-movement offense with elite assist creation and shot-making efficiency, deliberately trading offensive rebounds for floor balance and transition defense.
blueprints.offensive_system: PnR-centered advantage offense (Haliburton as initiator, Turner as roll-man), high passes/game, drive-and-kick rim pressure
blueprints.defensive_system: Approximately league-average; not scheme-driven; wins through offensive edge and clutch execution
construction_principles: Win through shot quality and decision speed, not volume rebounding — deliberately low ORR is a feature, not a bug
Protect the assist ratio: anti-fit prospects are those who spike turnovers without elite creation to compensate
Rim pressure + 3PT spacing is the shot diet (78% of attempts at rim or behind arc)
Clutch performance is defense-driven even though regular-season identity is offensive — need defenders who hold up when pace slows
Roster gap: needs at least one above-average rebounder at a core position to stabilize matchups

archetypes.label: Pass-first lead guard, advantage generator
fit_indicators: PnR Ball Handler PPP ≥ league median
anti_fit: high turnover rate without elite creation upside
archetypes.draft_note: System is assist-ratio-driven; must preserve this or offensive identity collapses

archetypes.label: Screen-and-roll big, above-average rebounder
fit_indicators: above-average rebounding to compensate team weakness
archetypes.draft_note: 290 roll-man possessions in 2024-25 — this is the highest-leverage non-star role in the system

archetypes.label: Quick-trigger shooter + secondary drive threat
archetypes.draft_note: Off-ball gravity matters more than creation here — system generates shots, needs someone to make them

structural_weaknesses: League-average defense — matchups where pace slows expose reliance on offensive edge
Bottom-tier offensive rebounding (21.3 ORR) means no second-chance cushion
Deep playoff runs require clutch defensive identity that regular-season metrics don't fully reveal

blueprints.result: 1st in West (60-16, regular season ongoing as of 2026-04-01)
blueprints.source_file: 2025 OKC Thunder Team Identity and Playstyle Report for Draft Modeling.pdf
blueprints.identity_sentence: Defense-first, turnover-forcing contender whose offense is driven by elite ball security and shot-making efficiency — wins by suffocating opponents, feeding offense from stops.
blueprints.offensive_system: Star-led half-court offense with connector big utility (Hartenstein short-roll/hub), secondary creators scaling when needed, midrange-capable star stabilizes late-clock
blueprints.defensive_system: High-pressure, event-driven, contest-quality oriented — forces turnovers and converts them into advantage offense; opponents shoot lowest FG% in NBA

stats.source: ESPN Hollinger + NBA official game notes (through 75 games), accessed 2026-04-01

construction_principles: Defense is not passive — it is event-generating: the goal is to force turnovers and convert them into advantage offense
Ball security is non-negotiable: league-best turnover% means every acquisition must prove mistake-avoidant decision-making
Roster fit is not positional — it's decision speed + defensive multiplicity (can they switch? can they pressure?)
Connector big role is critical: high-assist big (Hartenstein archetype) keeps possessions organized without creating turnover risk
Defense-first identity holds even with top-10 offense — proves the two aren't in conflict at roster construction level

archetypes.label: MVP-level on-ball engine, self-created efficiency
fit_indicators: low personal turnover rate despite high usage
archetypes.draft_note: System's offensive gravity depends entirely on this archetype — offense is top-10 because of SGA, not despite defense

archetypes.label: Wing who scales up as secondary pilot, doesn't compromise defense
fit_indicators: defensive effort + IQ at wing position
doesn't spike turnovers when usage increases
archetypes.draft_note: Offense remains top-10 specifically because this archetype exists — critical to not being one-dimensional

archetypes.label: Switchable, mobile big who contests without fouling
archetypes.draft_note: OKC tracks and explicitly cites Holmgren's contest results — this is a primary identity marker

archetypes.label: Switch/stunt/strip specialist, defensive multiplier
fit_indicators: amplifies team defense without needing offensive usage
anti_fit: defensive liability regardless of offensive value
archetypes.draft_note: Described as 'ceiling-raising' even in limited minutes — this archetype is a force multiplier on the No. 1 defense

archetypes.label: Screening + short-roll passing + rebounding utility
fit_indicators: low turnover rate for a passer
archetypes.draft_note: 9.7 REB + 3.7 AST explicitly cited in team notes — this is not a coincidence, it's a system requirement

archetypes.label: Pressure guard who fits the turnover-forcing loop
anti_fit: requires offensive usage to justify rotation spot
archetypes.draft_note: These are the pressure system's foot soldiers — must fit the deflection/turnover pipeline

structural_weaknesses: Moderate shot selection efficiency ('C' grade from secondary source) — relies on defense to compensate for below-elite shot diet
Youth-dependent depth — system continuity relies on young core continuing to develop

blueprints.result: Eastern Conference Contender (used as template/worked example in source doc)
blueprints.source_file: NBA team identity and playstyle profile for draft modeling.pdf
blueprints.identity_sentence: High-efficiency rim-and-3 offense anchored by elite shooting metrics and strong halfcourt execution, paired with a size-anchored defense.
blueprints.offensive_system: Rim-and-3 heavy halfcourt offense; efficient in transition despite slightly below-average frequency; shot-quality model rather than pace-driven
blueprints.defensive_system: Size-anchored defense; strong points-per-100 allowed; relies on physicality and length rather than pressure-forcing
construction_principles: Shooting efficiency and decision-making are primary — eFG% and TS% materially above league average means shot quality is the competitive edge
Turnover ratio better than league average — value low-mistake decision-makers and catch-and-shoot reliability at margins
Size anchors defense — physical, tall roster enables length-based rim protection and defensive rebounding
Pace is slightly above average but not extreme — offense wins through halfcourt quality, not pace exploitation
Above-average offensive rebounding (25.9 ORR vs 25.2 league) provides second-chance cushion

archetypes.label: Efficient primary scorer with low-turnover decision-making
fit_indicators: low turnover rate relative to usage
archetypes.draft_note: System is built on shot quality, not volume — this archetype must be efficient or the identity breaks

archetypes.label: Physical, tall big who rebounds and deters rim attempts
anti_fit: undersized big who cannot anchor defense
archetypes.draft_note: Defense is size-anchored, not scheme-driven — need physical presence to maintain defensive identity

archetypes.label: High-efficiency spot-up and off-movement shooter
archetypes.draft_note: Team's shot profile concentrates at rim and 3PT — wings must be able to punish from distance

structural_weaknesses: Below-average transition frequency despite efficient transition scoring — leaves possessions on the table
Defense relies on size, not pressure — vulnerable to pace teams that can outrun the size advantage
